process_and_outcome: The university's Student Conduct Office reviewed the case, imposing a failing grade for the course and a one-quarter suspension. The student appealed unsuccessfully, and the decision was publicized to educate the community on proper citation practices.

example_name: University of Missouri Fakes News Exposé
example_background: In 2019, a University of Missouri journalism professor publicly addressed students using AI-generated content and fake sources in assignments, compiling a report with examples of fabricated interviews and plagiarized articles passed off as original reporting. This occurred in a news writing class where students were expected to produce authentic stories based on real interviews.
process_and_outcome: The professor's report led to academic penalties for the involved students, including zeros on assignments and referrals to the academic integrity office. The university reinforced training on ethical journalism and AI use policies.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: University of Missouri Academic Integrity Policy
Society of Professional Journalists Code of Ethics

example_name: Tim Brady Whistleblower Case at Bentley University
example_background: Tim Brady, a student at Bentley University, discovered peers cheating on exams using online services and reported it to faculty in 2017. Despite initial inaction, he persisted by gathering evidence and escalating to administrators, highlighting systemic issues in proctoring during a high-stakes accounting exam.
process_and_outcome: The university investigated, disciplining the cheaters with suspensions. Brady was commended, receiving public recognition and contributing to policy reforms on reporting mechanisms and exam security.

example_name: Cameron Fetter Stanford Plagiarism Exposé
example_background: Stanford student Cameron Fetter identified and reported classmates submitting AI-generated essays as their own work in 2023, using detection tools to flag unnatural language patterns in humanities assignments. He compiled evidence over multiple courses and brought it to professors' attention to uphold standards.
process_and_outcome: Professors confirmed the issues, resulting in honor code violations for the students involved, including grade penalties. Fetter's actions led to campus-wide discussions and adoption of AI policies.

example_name: West Point Honor System Enforcement
example_background: The United States Military Academy at West Point maintains a strict honor system where cadets report peers' violations, such as unauthorized collaboration on assignments. In a 2021 case, cadets self-reported a group copying homework solutions, demonstrating the system's reliance on peer accountability in a rigorous academic environment.
process_and_outcome: The Honor Committee investigated, leading to separations from the academy for the involved cadets. The reporting cadets faced no reprisal and were upheld as models of integrity.
involved_parties: United States Military Academy West Point

example_name: Elizabeth Redden Academic Misconduct Case
example_background: Elizabeth Redden, a PhD student at the University of Western Australia, faced allegations in 2021 for submitting fabricated survey data in her honours thesis on childhood trauma. She invented participant responses and created fake online survey accounts to simulate data collection. Supervisors identified inconsistencies during routine checks, such as uniform response patterns and impossible demographic distributions. The case involved her thesis supervisor who initially approved the work before raising concerns.
process_and_outcome: The university's misconduct panel found her guilty after reviewing metadata and statistical anomalies. Redden received a 2.5-year suspension, had her degree rescinded, and was barred from re-enrollment. She appealed unsuccessfully, and the decision was publicized to deter similar actions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: University of Western Australia Academic Misconduct Procedures
Australian Code for the Responsible Conduct of Research

example_name: Stuyvesant High School Cheating Ring
example_background: In 2015, at New York City's elite Stuyvesant High School, a group of students operated a cheating network during Regents exams, sharing answers via GroupMe app and smartwatches. Over 50 students coordinated to photograph and distribute math and physics exam questions in real-time. Teachers reported suspicious behaviors like frequent bathroom breaks and phone usage. The scheme exploited high-stakes standardized testing pressures in a competitive academic environment.
process_and_outcome: New York Department of Education investigated with digital evidence and witness statements. Involved students faced exam invalidation, suspensions, and mandatory integrity training. School implemented stricter exam protocols including signal jammers.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: New York State Education Department Testing Regulations
NYC DOE Student Code of Conduct

example_name: Northwestern University Contract Cheating Bust
example_background: In 2019, Northwestern University discovered students using essay mills like EssayShark for assignments in writing and history courses. Detection came from stylistic inconsistencies flagged by professors and confirmed via Google reverse searches matching student papers to mill websites. Approximately 12 students were involved, paying up to $200 per paper during finals period.
process_and_outcome: Academic Integrity Office hearings resulted in grade reductions, probation, and one-year suspensions for repeat offenders. University partnered with Turnitin enhancements and educated faculty on contract cheating signs.

example_name: US Naval Academy Honor Concept Enforcement
example_background: The US Naval Academy maintains a strict Honor Concept where midshipmen pledge not to lie, cheat, or steal, with peers reporting violations. In 2020, during a calculus exam incident, several midshipmen self-reported collaboration, leading to group resignation offers. The brigade honor board emphasized collective responsibility over individual gain.
process_and_outcome: Honor board accepted resignations from seven midshipmen after deliberations, reinforcing the system's effectiveness. Academy-wide training sessions followed, maintaining a culture of discomfort for upholding beliefs.

example_name: University of Georgia Academic Misconduct Case (2011)
example_background: In 2011, more than 60 students at the University of Georgia were implicated in a widespread cheating scandal involving the sharing of answers for online quizzes and exams in an introductory astronomy course. The incident came to light after a student reported suspicions to the instructor, prompting a detailed investigation into unusual patterns of identical answers across multiple student submissions. The case involved undergraduate students who accessed shared answer keys through digital means, affecting the integrity of the course assessment process. This event highlighted vulnerabilities in online testing environments and the pressures students face in large lecture courses.
process_and_outcome: University officials conducted an investigation, confirmed the misconduct through data analysis, and imposed sanctions including academic probation, course failures, and suspensions ranging from one to four semesters for the involved students. The university also revised its online testing protocols to include proctoring and randomized question banks.

example_name: Harvard University Plagiarism Scandal (2012)
example_background: In 2012, Harvard College identified significant issues with plagiarism and collaboration beyond permitted levels in a government course taught by Professor Michael Sandel. Nearly half of the 279 students enrolled received warnings or were asked to withdraw from the course after submitting take-home exams that contained identical phrasing and analytical passages. The problems arose from students working together on assignments intended to be individual efforts, with some copying from uncited online sources and study aids. This incident occurred in a highly regarded institution and involved a popular course, drawing national attention to enforcement challenges.
process_and_outcome: An administrative board reviewed evidence from similarity detection software and student interviews, resulting in nearly half the class facing penalties: 9 students withdrew, 7 faced probation, and others received written warnings. The professor updated assignment guidelines, and the university enhanced its plagiarism detection training.

example_name: Stuyvesant High School Cheating Incident (2015)
example_background: In 2015, over 50 students at Stuyvesant High School in New York City were found to have shared answers to the SAT and AP exams through group messages and shared documents. The scheme involved students from multiple schools coordinating via digital platforms to exchange test questions during breaks or immediately after exams. This occurred in one of the nation's most competitive public high schools, where high-stakes testing pressures contributed to the coordinated effort among top-performing students.
process_and_outcome: The College Board and Educational Testing Service investigated using digital forensics and witness statements, voiding scores for 16 students and reporting others to schools. Stuyvesant disciplined participants with suspensions and mandatory ethics training, while test security protocols were strengthened nationwide.

example_name: Georgia Tech Online Course Cheating (2019)
example_background: During an online introduction to computing course at Georgia Institute of Technology in 2019, approximately 70 students were found to have engaged in unauthorized collaboration by sharing code solutions on external forums and through group chats. The massive open online course (MOOC) format amplified the issue, with thousands enrolled, but proctoring logs and code similarity analysis revealed patterns of identical submissions. This case underscored challenges in maintaining standards in scalable digital education environments.
process_and_outcome: University investigators used plagiarism detection tools and IP tracing, leading to failing grades for the course, academic probation, and referrals to the honor council for some students. Georgia Tech implemented improved proctoring software and randomized assignments across sections.

example_name: University of Central Florida Contract Cheating Scheme (2019)
example_background: Federal prosecutors uncovered a multimillion-dollar operation where an Indian company, Essay Mills, paid American students at the University of Central Florida to take online courses and exams for international students enrolled in business and IT programs. Between 2014 and 2019, proxy test-takers completed coursework, enabling degree completion without direct participation. The scheme exploited online program flexibility, involving dozens of U.S. students as proxies for clients paying thousands per course.
process_and_outcome: The FBI investigated following tips from university staff, leading to guilty pleas from key operators. The company forfeited $10.8 million, and implicated students faced expulsion. UCF enhanced identity verification for online students.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Higher Education Opportunity Act (contract cheating provisions)

example_name: UNC Chapel Hill Fake Classes Scandal (2011-2015)
example_background: From 1993 to 2015, the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill maintained over 3,100 sham courses, primarily in the Department of African and Afro-American Studies, where students were enrolled but never attended classes or took exams. Faculty assigned automatic high grades based on loosely submitted papers, benefiting over 3,500 students, including 47% athletes in revenue sports. The arrangement persisted through departmental changes and involved paper classes with irregular numbering and no instruction.
process_and_outcome: A 2012 investigation by faculty and external reports led to NCAA sanctions, including scholarship reductions and vacated wins. Criminal charges against a former tutor were dropped, but the university overhauled academic oversight, fired involved staff, and paid $3.5 million in settlements.
involved_parties: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
Department of African and Afro-American Studies

example_name: Stanford Honor Code Enforcement Success
example_background: Stanford University maintains a student-run Honor Code established in 1921, which relies on peer reporting and voluntary compliance to uphold academic standards. In various instances, such as during high-pressure exam periods, students have self-reported minor infractions like accidental unauthorized aid, leading to educational resolutions rather than punitive measures. This system fosters a culture of trust and personal responsibility across undergraduate programs.
process_and_outcome: The Honor Council reviews cases confidentially, often resulting in required ethics courses or grade adjustments, with low recidivism rates. Annual reports show effective deterrence, with thousands of exams annually conducted without proctors.

example_name: Georgia Tech Online Class Cheating Incident
example_background: In a massive online course on edX offered by Georgia Tech with over 8,000 participants, students accessed a hidden online forum where they shared solutions to quizzes and assignments. The forum, discovered by instructors, contained step-by-step answers and code snippets posted publicly. This occurred in a computer science course focused on data structures, where collaboration crossed into unauthorized aid. Instructors monitored analytics showing unusual patterns in submission timings and identical errors across submissions, leading to the forum's identification.
process_and_outcome: Around 70 students were identified and removed from the course. Georgia Tech issued warnings and adjusted grading to exclude affected assessments. The platform implemented better monitoring tools, and the case was used in faculty training sessions to improve detection methods.

example_name: University of Iowa Research Misconduct Case - Poehlman
example_background: Former University of Vermont professor Eric Poehlman fabricated data in multiple grant applications and publications while affiliated with the University of Iowa's research network. He invented patient data in studies on menopause and aging, submitting falsified results to NIH-funded projects. The misconduct spanned years and involved altering spreadsheets to support conclusions.
process_and_outcome: Office of Research Integrity investigated, leading to Poehlman's debarment from federal funding for 10 years and criminal conviction with prison time. Retracted papers and university oversight reforms followed.

example_name: Elizabeth Holmes Theranos Fraud
example_background: Elizabeth Holmes founded Theranos, claiming revolutionary blood-testing technology capable of hundreds of tests from drops of blood. She presented falsified demonstrations and data to investors and regulators, overstating device accuracy. Partnerships with Walgreens proceeded based on misleading validations, deceiving stakeholders about clinical validity.
process_and_outcome: SEC charged fraud, Holmes settled with penalties and was banned from serving as officer. Criminal trial in 2022 resulted in conviction on wire fraud counts, 11-year prison sentence. Company dissolved.

example_name: Stanford Honor Code System Success
example_background: Stanford University maintains one of the oldest student-run honor codes, established in 1921, requiring students to pledge not to give or receive unauthorized aid. The code fosters self-governance, with students reporting violations voluntarily. Annual surveys show high trust levels, with low cheating rates compared to peer institutions, attributed to community enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing audits confirm effectiveness, with few incidents annually. Code updates incorporate online learning safeguards, sustaining low violation rates.
